metadata.summary: Async/await patterns for Python 3.11+ I/O-bound services using TaskGroup, Semaphore, and app-scoped clients. Prevents blocking calls, resource exhaustion, and zombie tasks.

content.title: Python Async Patterns for I/O-Bound Services
content.summary: Async/await patterns for Python 3.11+ I/O-bound services. Uses asyncio.TaskGroup for structured concurrency, asyncio.Semaphore to bound fan-out, and app-scoped httpx.AsyncClient / DB pools. CPU-bound work goes to run_in_executor or ProcessPoolExecutor. Blocking calls inside async def (requests, time.sleep, psycopg2) are a hard violation.

why.p: A single blocking call inside an async function freezes the entire event loop for all concurrent requests. TaskGroup propagates cancellation and exceptions automatically, closing the zombie-task leak that plagues asyncio.gather. Bounding concurrency with Semaphore prevents resource exhaustion on downstreams.

list.item: FastAPI, Starlette, aiohttp, or Litestar services with high concurrent I/O (HTTP, DB, Redis, S3).
Fan-out/fan-in: dozens-to-thousands of concurrent external calls per request.
Long-lived connections: WebSocket, SSE, gRPC streaming, MQTT.
Background workers consuming Redis Streams / NATS / RabbitMQ.
CPU-bound workloads (ML inference, image processing) — async adds overhead without benefit.
One-off scripts where requests + threads is simpler and equivalent.
Codebases with deep sync dependencies (legacy ORMs, blocking SDKs) requiring run_in_executor everywhere.
Django request handlers without ASGI deployment — partial async serializes everything.
Python < 3.11 — TaskGroup and improved cancellation are the practical baseline.

rule.statement: Use asyncio.TaskGroup for any set of concurrent coroutines; prefer it over gather for new code on Python 3.11+.
rule.rationale: TaskGroup propagates cancellation automatically — if one task raises, all siblings are cancelled, preventing zombie tasks that gather leaves running.

example.code: async def process_all(items: list[str]) -> list[str]: results = [] async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg: tasks = [tg.create_task(process_item(item)) for item in items] # All tasks completed here; any exception cancels siblings return [t.result() for t in tasks]

rule.statement: Wrap every external-call fan-out with asyncio.Semaphore; derive the limit from downstream capacity, not an arbitrary number.
rule.rationale: Unbounded gather over a queue can spawn 100k tasks, consuming all RAM and crushing downstream services.

example.code:
async def fetch_all(urls: list[str], max_concurrent: int = 10) -> list[dict]: semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)
async def fetch_one(url: str) -> dict | None: async with semaphore: async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client: r = await client.get(url, timeout=30.0) r.raise_for_status() return r.json()
results = await asyncio.gather(*[fetch_one(u) for u in urls], return_exceptions=True) return [r for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception)]

rule.statement: Pair every await with a timeout using asyncio.timeout() (3.11+) or asyncio.wait_for(); never await without a deadline.
rule.rationale: Awaiting without a timeout means a stalled downstream silently hangs the event loop task indefinitely.

example.code: async def with_timeout(): try: async with asyncio.timeout(2.0): result = await slow_operation() return result except asyncio.TimeoutError: return None

rule.statement: Use async generators for paginated data streams to avoid loading all pages into memory.
rule.rationale: Loading all pages first wastes memory and delays first-item processing; generators yield items as they arrive.

example.code:
from typing import AsyncIterator
async def fetch_pages(base_url: str) -> AsyncIterator[dict]: page = 1 async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client: while True: r = await client.get(f"{base_url}?page={page}") data = r.json() if not data["items"]: break for item in data["items"]: yield item page += 1
async def process_stream(): async for item in fetch_pages("https://api.example.com/items"): await handle(item)

rule.statement: Create httpx.AsyncClient, DB pools, and Redis clients once at application startup; never instantiate them per request or inside loops.
rule.rationale: Creating a client per request leaks sockets, breaks HTTP/2 connection reuse, and exhausts file descriptors under load.

rule.statement: Implement resource cleanup via async context managers (asynccontextmanager or __aenter__/__aexit__); never rely on garbage collection for async resources.
rule.rationale: GC does not call async close() methods; connections and file handles leak silently without explicit context management.

example.code:
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
@asynccontextmanager async def get_db_session(): session = await create_session() try: yield session await session.commit() except Exception: await session.rollback() raise finally: await session.close()

rule.statement: Store all fire-and-forget tasks in a set and register a done callback to remove them; never discard the task reference immediately after create_task().
rule.rationale: Unreferenced tasks may be garbage-collected before completion; exceptions are silently swallowed.

rule.statement: Run CPU-bound operations in loop.run_in_executor(None, fn) for tasks under ~50ms; use ProcessPoolExecutor for longer tasks.
rule.rationale: A CPU-bound synchronous call inside async def blocks the event loop for all concurrent tasks for its full duration.

example.code:
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
_pool = ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)
async def process_image(data: bytes) -> bytes: loop = asyncio.get_running_loop() return await loop.run_in_executor(_pool, cpu_transform, data)

antipattern.description: Calling sync I/O (requests.get, time.sleep, psycopg2.connect) inside async def.
antipattern.reason: Blocks the entire event loop for all concurrent tasks for the full duration of the call.

example.code: # BAD: blocks event loop async def fetch_data(): response = requests.get(URL) # sync HTTP call return response.json()
# GOOD: async client async def fetch_data(): async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client: response = await client.get(URL) return response.json()

antipattern.description: Calling a coroutine without await: result = fetch_data() instead of result = await fetch_data().
antipattern.reason: The coroutine is created but never executed; the call silently does nothing and returns a coroutine object.

antipattern.description: Fire-and-forget with asyncio.create_task() without storing the reference.
antipattern.reason: Tasks without strong references may be garbage-collected mid-execution; exceptions are silently swallowed.

antipattern.description: Catching Exception (or bare except) in async code, which includes CancelledError on Python 3.7; swallowing it prevents graceful shutdown.
antipattern.reason: CancelledError must propagate for TaskGroup and timeout() to cancel correctly. On 3.8+ CancelledError no longer inherits Exception, but agents use old patterns.

antipattern.description: Creating httpx.AsyncClient() inside a request handler or loop iteration.
antipattern.reason: Each instantiation opens a new connection pool; leaks sockets and breaks HTTP/2 keep-alive. App-scoped singleton is required.

rule.statement: Treat CancelledError as control flow: catch only to perform cleanup, then re-raise unconditionally.
rule.rationale: Swallowing CancelledError prevents TaskGroup and timeout() from completing cancellation, leaving zombie tasks.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A four-stage pattern. (1) Boundary check: an agent classifies each new function as I/O-bound, CPU-bound, or mixed; CPU work goes to run_in_executor / concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor. (2) Concurrency primitive: agent picks TaskGroup for structured concurrency, gather only when fire-and-forget, with mandatory asyncio.Semaphore to bound concurrency on any external call. (3) Resource lifecycle: agent ensures shared httpx.AsyncClient / DB pool / Redis client are app-scoped, not request-scoped. (4) Test gate: an agent emits both happy-path and cancellation tests (asyncio.timeout, task.cancel()); coverage tool flags any async function without a cancel path.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent gates no requests, time.sleep, or psycopg2 in async modules; every external call has a semaphore; every TaskGroup has a timeout. A sync-leak detector subagent (worth creating) scans async modules with AST for blocking calls (requests.*, time.sleep, open() without aiofiles, psycopg2.*, boto3 without aiobotocore); returns line numbers + remediation. A TaskGroup converter rewrites legacy gather patterns into TaskGroup for Python 3.11+, preserving semantics. faion-feature-executor (skill) sequences design → boundary check → impl → cancellation test → load test.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Standardize on Python 3.11+ and use asyncio.TaskGroup for any concurrent set; it propagates cancellation and exceptions correctly. Bound every external-call fan-out with asyncio.Semaphore. Unbounded gather is an outage waiting to happen. Reuse a single httpx.AsyncClient per app (or per host pool); creating a client per request leaks sockets and breaks HTTP/2. Use asyncpg over psycopg2; for SQLAlchemy pick create_async_engine and async sessions, never mix sync + async sessions. Run CPU work in loop.run_in_executor(None, fn) (default thread pool) for short tasks; use ProcessPoolExecutor for tasks > ~50ms. Always pair await calls with timeouts: asyncio.timeout(5) (3.11+) wraps any block. Propagate context with contextvars. Logging libraries (structlog, loguru) and OTel use this; ad-hoc globals don't survive await. Use anyio if you need to support both asyncio and trio, or if you want better cancellation semantics. Add --debug event-loop in dev (PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1); it surfaces forgotten awaits and slow callbacks. Profile with py-spy --idle or aiomonitor; standard sampling profilers under-attribute event-loop time. Treat CancelledError as control flow: catch only to clean up, then re-raise.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Agents add async def everywhere even in CPU code, expecting performance — all that gives you is overhead and event-loop blocking. Force the I/O-vs-CPU classification step. LLMs love asyncio.run inside library code; this breaks composition (only the entry script should call it). Lint for it. Agents create httpx.AsyncClient() per call inside loops; require an app-scoped fixture/dep. They mix requests and httpx in the same async function; reject unconditionally in async modules. Agents forget to cancel children on parent timeout → "shielded zombies". Prefer TaskGroup so cancellation is automatic. Trio / anyio confusion: agents import trio.sleep while using asyncio. Pin the runtime in the system prompt. Tests pass under pytest-asyncio mode auto but break under strict; agents don't surface this. Pin mode and CI. Concurrency limits hallucinated: agents pick Semaphore(100) arbitrarily. Require the limit to be derived from downstream capacity (max conns / max QPS) and documented. Logging context lost: agents use print or non-context-aware loggers in async code; require structlog with contextvars integration. Cancellation mishandling: agents catch Exception (which includes CancelledError on 3.7) and swallow shutdowns. On 3.8+ CancelledError no longer inherits Exception; some agents are still using older patterns. Human-in-loop checkpoint: any change to global semaphores, pool sizes, or the event-loop policy must be human-approved with a load-test result.

section.name: cli-tools-services
section.p: pytest-asyncio: Async test support (pip install pytest-asyncio). anyio: Cross-runtime async (asyncio + trio) (pip install anyio). httpx: Async HTTP client (request/response parity with requests) (pip install httpx). aiohttp: Async HTTP server + client (pip install aiohttp). aiofiles: Async filesystem access (pip install aiofiles). aiobotocore / aioboto3: Async AWS SDK (pip install aioboto3). aiocache: Async caching primitives (pip install aiocache). aiomonitor: Inspect running event loop (pip install aiomonitor). asyncpg: Postgres async driver (fastest) (pip install asyncpg). aiosqlite: SQLite async wrapper (pip install aiosqlite). uvloop: Fast event loop replacement (Linux/macOS) (pip install uvloop). arq / dramatiq (with asyncio backend): Async background workers (pip install arq). faulthandler / tracemalloc: Diagnose hangs and leaks (stdlib).

FastAPI: OSS framework — code-first, standard async API stack. Litestar: OSS framework — faster than FastAPI for some workloads, similar API. Starlette: OSS framework — the toolkit FastAPI is built on. Uvicorn / Hypercorn / Granian: OSS ASGI servers — production runners; pick one and stick. Sentry: SaaS SDK — Sentry's async SDK propagates trace context. Datadog APM: SaaS SDK — auto-instruments asyncio + FastAPI. OpenTelemetry Python (auto-instrument): OSS CLI — opentelemetry-instrument uvicorn.... Grafana Tempo / Jaeger: OSS trace backends; pair with OTel. RabbitMQ / NATS / Kafka: OSS async clients — all have asyncio-native clients.

templates.template: AST scanner — finds blocking calls (requests, time.sleep, psycopg2) inside async def.
